The covenant people are always, in some sense, a threat to the kingdoms of this world.
1.10: Pharaoh's shrewdness deployed against the covenant people — the wisdom of this age confronting the divine purpose. Paul reflects on this pattern in 1 Cor 2:6-8: the rulers of this age did not understand the wisdom of God; if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory. The very instrument by which the earthly powers tried to eliminate the threat (crucifixion) became the means of their defeat. Pharaoh's shrewd plan to stop Israel produced the exact outcome he feared; Pilate's verdict accomplished the exact purpose he sought to prevent.
1.11: Israel labors to build Egypt's store cities — the covenant people's labor under the world's authority. A shadow: the Servant of Isaiah 52-53 is 'despised and rejected,' 'oppressed and afflicted,' bearing burdens not his own. Jesus identifies himself with the laboring and heavy-laden (Matt 11:28) precisely because the Son of God entered the condition of servitude to liberate those under it. The one who called Israel out of Egypt's service calls those under sin's burden to himself.
1.12: The more they were afflicted, the more they multiplied — the paradox of the indestructible covenant community. This principle governs the entire NT mission: persecution spreads the gospel (Acts 8:4); suffering produces endurance (Rom 5:3-4); death produces life (John 12:24). The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church (Tertullian). Pharaoh's attempt to reduce Israel produces the numerical growth that makes his fear self-fulfilling — exactly as the authorities' attempt to suppress the early church produced its expansion.
1.13: Crushing labor (abodah parach) — the same term later forbidden in Israel's own slave law (Lev 25:43). A shadow: the Servant of Isaiah 53:4-5 carries grief and sorrows, is stricken and crushed (medakka) — the same crushing that characterizes Egypt's oppression is the suffering the Servant takes on for others. Christ takes on the crushing that Israel suffered from Egypt, and the crushing that all humanity suffers under sin, bearing it in himself.
1.14: Bitter labor in mortar and brick and all field service — the bitterness of Egypt, memorialized in the bitter herbs of the Passover feast. At Gethsemane, Jesus asks if the cup of bitterness can pass from him (Matt 26:39) — the cup that Israel drank under Pharaoh becomes the cup the Servant drinks on behalf of all. The word Gethsemane means 'olive press' — the place of crushing. The bitterness of Egypt's service is contained within the bitterness of Gethsemane's prayer.
1.15: The midwives receive the death decree against Hebrew boys — the first of two death decrees in the Exodus narrative (the second in v.22 extends to all Egypt). A type: the decree to kill male infants at birth is the exact form of Herod's response to news of the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem (Matt 2:16). Matthew quotes Jer 31:15 ('Rachel weeping for her children') to link the two events explicitly — the slaughter at Bethlehem is the Exodus pattern repeated in the fullness of time.
1.16: A type: the death decree against Hebrew boys by the earthly king parallels Herod's massacre of the innocents (Matt 2:16-18) with structural precision: a powerful ruler fears the coming deliverer, issues a death decree against male infants, and the deliverer escapes while others die. Matthew explicitly connects the two events by citing Jer 31:15. The decree intended to prevent the deliverer's emergence is the backdrop against which the deliverer's miraculous preservation is displayed.
1.17: The midwives feared God rather than the king — the first act of obedience to divine authority over human authority in redemptive history. The NT principle stated in Acts 5:29 ('We must obey God rather than human beings') has its founding moment here. The midwives who protected the infant Israelites under a death decree are the counterparts of those who protect and serve Jesus despite hostile authority — Joseph, who hid the family in Egypt; the magi, who did not report to Herod (Matt 2:12).
1.18: Pharaoh interrogates the midwives about their disobedience — the covenant community called to account before earthly power for protecting what God is doing. Theme: the pattern recurs throughout the NT (Acts 4:7: 'By what power or what name did you do this?'). Those who stand before authority to answer for faithfulness to God participate in the same witness the midwives gave — calm, truthful, unafraid.
1.19: The midwives' reply — the Hebrew women are vigorous and give birth before they arrive. Theme: the life-force of the covenant community outpaces the reach of the death decree. The resurrection is the ultimate statement of this theme: the death decree of sin and law (Rom 6:23) is outrun by the life of God in Christ. Death cannot keep what Christ has made alive (Acts 2:24: 'it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him').
1.20: God was kind to the midwives and the people multiplied — divine blessing on faithful disobedience to a death decree. A revelation of God: YHWH rewards those who protect the vulnerable and honor him above earthly power. This divine character — rewarding those who align with God's purposes over against earthly authority — is fully expressed in Christ's beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12: blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness).
1.21: God gave the midwives families of their own — life for those who gave life. The principle of seed and harvest (Gal 6:7-8) is illustrated in this reward: the midwives preserved the lives of Hebrew children; God gives them children. The theme of resurrection-reward — receiving back what was risked for God's sake — runs through the NT (Mark 10:29-30: 'no one who has left... for me and the gospel will fail to receive a hundred times as much').
1.22: A type: Pharaoh's edict to throw every Hebrew boy into the Nile directly parallels Herod's massacre of the innocents (Matt 2:16-18). The NT does not make the connection allegorically — Matthew does it textually, citing Jer 31:15 to show that both events are part of the same redemptive pattern. In both, an earthly king fears the deliverer and acts with mass violence; in both, the deliverer is preserved while the innocent die. The preserved deliverer carries the weight of the innocent who died for his sake.

2.1: A Levite man marries a Levite woman — the deliverer will come from the priestly tribe. The NT notes that Jesus belongs to the royal tribe (Judah/David) through Joseph, but Luke 1:36 identifies Elizabeth (mother of John the Baptist) as a daughter of Aaron, and Elizabeth and Mary are relatives — meaning Jesus has both royal and priestly lineage in his extended family. The priestly and royal vocations that were constitutionally separate in the Mosaic order converge in Christ (Heb 7:14-16).
2.2: She saw that he was good (ki tov) — the creation goodness formula (Gen 1:4, 10, 12, etc.) applied to the child. A shadow: the Incarnation is the arrival of the one who is the image of God (2 Cor 4:4; Col 1:15) — the one in whom the goodness of creation is fully expressed and from whom it flows. Joseph and Mary hide the child from Herod for two years (Matt 2:13-15) just as the mother hides Moses for three months — the preservation of the deliverer by those who recognize his significance.
2.3: The tevah (basket/ark) placed in the Nile waters — a type with two layers. First, the tevah (used only here and in Gen 6-9 for Noah's ark) signals that Moses is a new Noah: preserved in a vessel on threatening waters while death surrounds. Second, the infant placed in the waters who emerges as deliverer anticipates the Baptism pattern — Jesus enters the Jordan's waters (Matt 3:13-17), is identified as God's Son, and emerges to lead his people through the wilderness. Both Noah's ark and the baptismal waters accomplish preservation through death-threatening water.
2.4: Miriam watches from a distance — the faithful witness at the water, positioned at the margin of events. A shadow: women watch Jesus from a distance at the cross (Matt 27:55-56), and women are the first witnesses at the empty tomb (Matt 28:1-10). The marginal witness — female, young, powerless — is consistently the first and most faithful observer of the deliverer's vulnerability and triumph throughout redemptive history.
2.5: Pharaoh's daughter descends to the Nile to bathe and discovers the basket — providence arranges the meeting of the condemned child with the one who has power to save him. A revelation of God: YHWH uses the unexpected, the Gentile, the powerful-for-worldly-purposes to accomplish his purpose. The Magi (Gentile astronomers) come to honor Jesus; the Roman centurion confesses him at the cross (Matt 27:54). Those outside the covenant people often recognize and protect the deliverer.
2.6: She had compassion on him — vatachmol, from the same root as racham (womb-love, compassion). A revelation of God: the character of divine compassion that moves YHWH to deliver Israel (Ex 3:7: 'I know their sufferings') is expressed through a human being who sees, recognizes, and has compassion on a condemned child. Jesus is consistently described as moved with compassion (esplagchnisthe — Matt 9:36, 14:14, 15:32, 20:34), seeing what others pass by. The compassion of Pharaoh's daughter is a creaturely reflection of the divine compassion that became incarnate in Christ.
2.7: Miriam's initiative — arranging for Moses' own mother to nurse him at Pharaoh's expense. A shadow: the providential reversal of the death decree through human initiative and divine orchestration. The Incarnation is the ultimate such reversal: what the powers of this world intended as defeat (the cross) becomes the means of cosmic victory. The deliverer is preserved and nourished by the very household that sought his death.
2.8: Pharaoh's daughter accepts Miriam's offer — the authority that condemned now sponsors. A shadow: the Roman court that condemned Jesus (crucifixion under Pontius Pilate, a Roman legal execution) has its verdict reversed when a Roman soldier confesses him as the Son of God (Matt 27:54) and the empire eventually adopts the faith. The household of the oppressor becomes the context in which the deliverer is formed — Joseph's imprisonment by Potiphar becomes his path to Pharaoh's court; Jesus' death under Roman authority becomes the basis of the Roman church.
2.9: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages' — the mother of the condemned child is paid by the household of condemnation to raise her son. A revelation of God: divine irony as the mode of grace. What the world intends as final becomes the condition of formation. The wisdom of God is foolishness to the world (1 Cor 1:25); the power of God is displayed in what appears weak. Jochebed, paid by Pharaoh's court to nurse the future liberator of Israel, embodies this principle at the personal level.
2.10: Moses is raised in the Egyptian palace — forty years of formation in the household of the oppressor. Acts 7:22 adds: 'Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and was powerful in speech and action.' A shadow: Jesus grows up hidden in Nazareth, his messianic identity concealed while he is formed through ordinary human life, labor, and learning (Luke 2:52: he grew in wisdom and stature). The deliverer's public identity is preceded by decades of hidden formation that the world neither sees nor values.
2.11: Moses goes out to see his brothers' suffering and identifies with them — the moment of identification. A type: the Incarnation is this moment raised to cosmic scale. John 1:14: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' Jesus goes out to where his people are, takes on their condition (Phil 2:6-8: 'being found in human form, he humbled himself'). Moses identifies with the Hebrews by going where they are; the eternal Son identifies with humanity by becoming what we are.
2.12: Moses kills the Egyptian and hides him in the sand — the failed first rescue, the unauthorized act of justice. A shadow of the contrast with Christ: Moses acts without commission, in secret, and the result is flight rather than liberation. Jesus acts with full divine authorization, openly, and the result is completed redemption. Moses' failed attempt highlights what is distinctive about Christ's: not impulsive but sent; not hidden but public; not partial but complete (Heb 3:3-6: Jesus worthy of more glory than Moses).
2.13: Moses is rejected by a Hebrew who asks 'Who made you a ruler and judge over us?' — a direct citation by Stephen in Acts 7:27, 35 as the pattern repeated with Jesus. Stephen explicitly argues: this is how Israel treats its sent deliverers. The one raised up by God to rescue them is first rejected by the very people he was sent to save; only after rejection, suffering, and divine vindication is he recognized as the appointed deliverer. Stephen uses this argument to explain the crucifixion: Israel rejected Jesus as they rejected Moses at first.
2.14: A type, explicitly drawn in Acts 7:27, 35: 'Who made you ruler and judge?' — the rejection of the sent deliverer by those he was sent to save. Stephen's argument in Acts 7:25-27 is that Moses is the prototype of the rejected and then vindicated deliverer: 'He assumed that his own people would understand that God was using him to rescue them, but they did not.' The NT uses this rejection-then-vindication pattern as the interpretive framework for the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus.
2.15: Moses flees to Midian and spends forty years as a shepherd in the wilderness. A shadow: the forty years in Midian correspond structurally to Jesus' forty days in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11). Both involve withdrawal, testing, and formation before the public commissioning that inaugurates the mission of deliverance. Both figures emerge from the wilderness experience to confront the powers of their day. The wilderness is the school of the deliverer — the place where human resources are stripped away and divine provision is the only ground to stand on.
2.16: Moses meets the seven daughters of Jethro at the well in Midian — the shepherd who defends the vulnerable at the place of water. A shadow: Jesus meets the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well (John 4:6-26), offering living water and identifying himself as the promised Messiah. The well-meeting scene is a patriarchal type-scene (Isaac/Rebekah, Jacob/Rachel) that culminates in Jesus' conversation at the well — the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) meets the excluded at the water.
2.17: Moses drives away the male shepherds and waters the flock for Jethro's daughters — the shepherd protects the vulnerable against exploitation. A type: Jesus drives out those who exploit the temple (Matt 21:12-13), cleansing the place of divine encounter from commercial predation. The Good Shepherd protects the sheep from the hireling who abandons them (John 10:11-13). Moses' protective act at the Midianite well is a miniature of the Good Shepherd's defining character.
2.18: Jethro's daughters report 'an Egyptian man delivered us' — the deliverer's identity is hidden behind his appearance. Moses looks Egyptian; he is Hebrew. A shadow: Jesus is identified as a Galilean carpenter (Mark 6:3), his divine identity hidden behind human appearance. Those who receive him are those who look past the apparent and recognize the real (Matt 16:16-17: Peter's confession — 'You are the Messiah' — revealed by the Father, not by flesh and blood).
2.19: The deliverer rescues the daughters from hostile shepherds and provides water for their flock. A revelation of God: the same divine character that acts on behalf of the powerless against the powerful — the theme of Ex 3:7-10 — is expressed in Moses' act at the well. The God who hears the cry of the oppressed in Egypt acts through Moses at a Midianite well. Jesus' ministry is the Incarnation of this divine disposition: he heals on the Sabbath, eats with sinners, touches lepers — the God who sides with the vulnerable, present in human form.
2.20: Jethro's hospitality invitation — the priest of Midian recognizes the deliverer and brings him in. A shadow: Cornelius (Acts 10), the Roman centurion who receives the gospel first among the Gentiles, mirrors Jethro — a non-Israelite who receives the sent one with hospitality and whose household becomes the first Gentile church. The trajectory of the gospel — from Israel to the nations — is anticipated in Moses' sojourn in Midian and his reception into a Gentile priestly household.
2.21: Moses marries Zipporah — the deliverer takes a bride from among the nations. A theme: Christ takes a bride from among all nations — the church as the bride of Christ (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7-9). As Moses received Zipporah (a Midianite/Gentile) as his bride after fleeing Egypt, so Christ's bride is drawn from all peoples, languages, and nations (Rev 5:9-10). The Gentile bride of the covenant mediator is a recurring type: Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba in Matthew's genealogy (1:5-6), and Zipporah here.
2.22: 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land' — Gershom. The deliverer lives as an alien, belonging fully to no earthly nation. A shadow: 'The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matt 8:20). Jesus is the ultimate Gershom — the stranger in the world he made (John 1:10-11: 'the world did not recognize him... his own did not receive him'). The deliverer's estrangement from every earthly home is the condition that enables him to be the home of all who have no home.
2.23: The Israelites groan and cry out — the legal appeal (za'aqah) reaching the divine throne. A revelation of God that culminates in Christ: the God who hears the cry of the oppressed is the God who in Christ identifies himself as the one to whom the weary and heavy-laden should cry (Matt 11:28). Jesus himself cries out from the cross ('Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani' — Mark 15:34), entering the position of the abandoned crier and transforming it from the inside. The God who hears the cry of Ex 2:23 becomes in Christ the one who cries it.
2.24: God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the covenant-memory as the engine of redemption. A direct theological foundation: Paul argues in Gal 3:15-18 and Rom 4 that God's covenant with Abraham, confirmed 430 years before the law, is the ground of salvation — and this is the covenant YHWH remembers here. What God remembers in Ex 2:24 he fulfills in Christ: 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him' (2 Cor 1:20). The Exodus is the first installment of a redemption that finds its final expression in the cross and resurrection.
2.25: God saw Israel and was concerned — four acts of divine attention (heard, remembered, saw, knew) constitute the prelude to divine action. A revelation of God that culminates in the Incarnation: the God who sees, hears, remembers, and knows the condition of his people does not remain at a distance. In Christ, divine attention becomes divine presence: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14) — the God of Ex 2:25 comes himself to be with those whose condition he knows. The four verbs of attention become the one act of permanent arrival.

3.1: Moses in the far wilderness — the future deliverer tending another man's flock in obscurity. A shadow: Jesus spent thirty years in Nazareth in carpenter's work before his public commissioning. The deliverer's public ministry is preceded by obscure, patient labor. Deut 18:15's promise of a prophet like Moses — the promise grounding the entire OT prophetic tradition — is fulfilled by the one who began, like Moses, in an unrecognized shepherding work before the divine commission came.
3.2: The burning bush — fire that burns without consuming, the theophany at the mountain. A shadow and type: the Transfiguration (Matt 17:1-8) is the NT burning bush — the divine glory blazing through Jesus' human form, the fire of deity burning in the bush of flesh without consuming it. In both events, the disciples/Moses are terrified and fall on their faces; in both, a divine voice commissions the witness. The bush that burns but is not consumed is the visual form of the Incarnation: the eternal fire of divine holiness dwelling in mortal flesh without destroying it.
3.3: Moses turns aside to investigate — the curiosity that opens into commissioning. A revelation of God: the divine approach is gentle, inviting investigation rather than commanding from a distance. Jesus invites approach in exactly the same way: 'Come to me' (Matt 11:28), 'Follow me' (Mark 1:17), 'Come and see' (John 1:39). The burning bush does not blast Moses from afar; it draws him near and waits. The divine initiative that invites rather than coerces is the form of grace throughout Scripture.
3.4: 'Moses, Moses' — the double personal naming, the form of divine call. Throughout the NT, Jesus calls individuals by name: 'Mary' (John 20:16 — the resurrection encounter), 'Saul, Saul' (Acts 9:4 — the Damascus road), 'Simon, Simon' (Luke 22:31 — the warning). The double calling in the OT (Abraham, Abraham in Gen 22:11; Samuel, Samuel in 1 Sam 3:10) signals sovereign attention and urgent commission. 'Here I am' (hineni) — the response of total availability: the form of election is always this exchange of divine address and human response.
3.5: 'Remove your sandals — the place where you stand is holy ground.' In John 1:27, John the Baptist declares he is not worthy to remove the sandal of the one coming after him. The command at the burning bush reverses: Moses removes his sandals before the presence of YHWH; John refuses to remove the sandal of Jesus because Jesus' presence far exceeds the category that requires sandal-removal. The one before whom Moses removes his sandals is the one before whom John cannot presume to act as a slave.
3.6: A direct Christological argument: Jesus cites this verse in Matt 22:32 / Mark 12:26 to prove the resurrection of the dead. His logic: YHWH says 'I am the God of Abraham' (present tense) — therefore Abraham is alive before God at the moment Moses stands at the bush. YHWH is not God of the dead but of the living. The burning bush is therefore not merely the site of Moses' commission but the scriptural proof, in Jesus' own teaching, that death does not end the relationship between YHWH and his covenant people.
3.7: 'I have surely seen... I have heard... I know their sufferings' — the threefold divine attention. A revelation of God: the compassionate, responsive God who sees and knows the suffering of his people is the God who becomes incarnate precisely to share and bear that suffering. Isa 53:3-4: the Servant is 'a man of suffering, and familiar with pain... he took up our pain and bore our suffering.' The God of Ex 3:7 does not merely see from above; in Christ he descends into the suffering he sees. The Incarnation is the fullest form of YHWH's 'I know their sufferings.'
3.8: A near-direct citation: 'I have come down to deliver them and to bring them up out of that land.' Jesus says in John 6:38: 'For I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me.' The vocabulary is the same: divine descent, purposive delivery, bringing up and out. John's entire prologue (1:1-18) is structured around the pattern announced in Ex 3:8: the descent of the divine to deliver and bring up. The Exodus is not merely a historical parallel — it is the conceptual template for the Incarnation.
3.9: The cry of the Israelites has reached YHWH and he has seen their oppression — the legal appeal received and acknowledged. A revelation of God: the God who attends to the cry of the powerless is the God whose Son responds to every cry of need in the gospels. Blind Bartimaeus calls out and is not silenced (Mark 10:46-52); the ten lepers cry from a distance (Luke 17:11-13); Jairus falls at Jesus' feet (Mark 5:22-23). The God who heard the cry of enslaved Israel hears every human cry through the one in whom he became fully human.
3.10: 'Come, I will send you to Pharaoh' — the commissioning of Moses as shaliach (sent one). A type: Jesus is consistently identified as the one sent by the Father (John 3:17, 5:24, 6:38-40, 17:3). The chain of sending runs from Ex 3:10 through the apostolate: 'As the Father has sent me, I am sending you' (John 20:21). Deut 18:15 promises that YHWH will raise up a prophet like Moses — one who speaks YHWH's words as his commissioned representative, the ultimate sent one whose words are the Father's own (John 14:10).
3.11: 'Who am I?' — Moses' objection based on inadequacy. A revelation of God by contrast: Jesus never asks 'who am I?' His identity is given, not sought (Matt 3:17: 'This is my Son, whom I love'). Moses points forward to the one whose commission requires no qualification — not 'who am I to go?' but 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30). Heb 3:1-6 makes the comparison explicit: Moses was faithful in God's house as a servant; Jesus is faithful as the Son over the house. The servant's inadequacy points to the Son's sufficiency.
3.12: The sign that comes after, not before — faith required before evidence arrives. A shadow: the structure of all Christian discipleship mirrors this pattern. Jesus calls the disciples to follow before they fully understand (Mark 1:17-20); the resurrection is the retrospective sign that confirms the mission, not the advance proof that enables it. Heb 11:27 (Moses) and the whole gallery of faith in Heb 11 are structured around this pattern: acting on the unseen promise, receiving the confirmation after. The burning bush sign is the template of faith.
3.13: Moses asks what name to give when Israel asks who sent him — the demand for divine identification. The question anticipates the NT's central dispute about Jesus' identity. 'Who are you?' is asked of Jesus repeatedly (John 8:25, Mark 11:28). His answer, grounded in the divine name revealed here, is the claim that divides: 'I am' (John 8:58). The question of Ex 3:13 — by whose authority and in whose name? — is the question the religious authorities ask of Jesus at every turn (Mark 11:28: 'By what authority are you doing these things?').
3.14: A direct and foundational Christological text: 'I AM WHO I AM' (Ehyeh asher ehyeh) — the divine name revealed to Moses at the burning bush. Jesus applies the absolute 'I am' (ego eimi) to himself in John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I am' — the crowd recognizes the claim and takes up stones (John 8:59). The seven 'I am' statements in John (bread of life, light of the world, gate, good shepherd, resurrection and life, way/truth/life, true vine) are elaborations of what the name means concretely. The divine name of Ex 3:14 is the name Jesus claims as his own throughout John's Gospel.
3.15: YHWH as the covenant name, the name that is memorial throughout all generations — a direct connection to the NT's treatment of the name of Jesus. Phil 2:9-11: God gave him 'the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.' Acts 4:12: 'there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved.' The name YHWH that was Israel's exclusive covenant memorial (Ex 3:15) is, in the NT's understanding, borne by Jesus — the one in whom YHWH's character and covenant are fully disclosed and embodied.
3.16: Gather the elders of Israel — Moses convenes Israel's representative leadership before his mission begins. A type: Jesus gathers the twelve apostles as the representative reconstitution of Israel's twelve tribes (Matt 19:28). The assembly of the elders in Ex 3:16 is the OT counterpart of the calling of the twelve: in both cases, the deliverer's first act is to constitute a representative body through whom the covenant community will be organized and the mission carried out.
3.17: The land of milk and honey — the inheritance promised to Abraham and now being actualized. A shadow: the promised land is a type of the ultimate inheritance of the people of God. Heb 11:13-16 makes this explicit — the patriarchs welcomed the promises from afar and acknowledged they were strangers on the earth, looking forward to a better country, the heavenly one. 1 Pet 1:4 describes the Christian inheritance as 'imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, reserved in heaven for you' — what Canaan was typologically, the new creation is permanently. Jesus comes to give his people not a land but himself as their dwelling (John 14:2-3).
3.18: 'Let us go a three-day journey to sacrifice to YHWH our God' — the three-day journey for sacrifice. A shadow: the three-day pattern in the OT is consistently connected to deliverance and resurrection (Gen 22:4 — Abraham sees the mountain on the third day; Jonah 1:17 — three days in the fish; Hos 6:2 — on the third day he will restore us). Jesus applies the Jonah sign explicitly to his own death and resurrection (Matt 12:40). The three-day journey to the place of sacrifice in Ex 3:18 is a small shadow of the three days between the cross and the empty tomb.
3.19: YHWH foreknows that Pharaoh will resist — the hardened heart of the earthly ruler. A revelation of God: YHWH's purposes are not blocked by human resistance; they are accomplished through it. Rom 9:17 applies this directly: 'I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you' (citing Ex 9:16). The hardening of Pharaoh's heart demonstrates that the powers of this age cannot finally resist the divine purpose — the same principle Paul applies to the cross: the rulers of this age crucified the Lord of glory, thus unknowingly accomplishing the purpose they sought to prevent (1 Cor 2:8).
3.20: The wonders YHWH will perform against Egypt — the signs and miracles that authenticate the divine commission and break the power of the oppressor. A theme: Jesus performs signs and wonders that authenticate his messianic commission (John 10:25, 20:30-31). The purpose of both the Exodus signs (demonstrating YHWH's sovereignty over Egypt's gods) and Jesus' signs (demonstrating the kingdom's arrival) is the same: to show that the power of the deliverer exceeds the power of every opposing force. The plagues dethrone Pharaoh; the miracles of Jesus dethrone the power of sin, disease, and death.
3.21: YHWH will cause the Egyptians to look favorably on Israel — divine reversal of the social order. A revelation of God: the God who reverses social hierarchies in favor of the covenant community. The Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) is the NT statement of this principle: 'he has scattered those who are proud... he has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble... he has filled the hungry with good things but has sent the rich away empty.' What YHWH does for Israel in Egypt, he does definitively for all humanity in Christ.
3.22: The plundering of Egypt — Israel leaves with silver, gold, and clothing from their Egyptian neighbors. A shadow: Col 2:15 describes the cross as the plundering of the powers and authorities: 'Having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross.' What Egypt's wealth was to Israel's exodus, the spoils of the cross are to the church's liberation: the very resources of the enemy become the endowment of the delivered people. The exodus that began with a death decree ends with the covenant community enriched at the oppressor's expense.

4.1: Moses's first objection to his commission: 'What if they do not believe me?' anticipates the perennial problem of divine credentialing. Jesus also faced the demand for signs that authenticate divine mission (John 2:18; Matt 12:38-39). But where Moses needed external signs to validate his word, Jesus himself is the Word: his signs authenticate not a message about God but his own person as the Son of God (John 20:30-31). The credentials question is answered definitively in the resurrection.
4.2: The staff in Moses's hand — a shepherd's tool — becomes the instrument of miraculous works. The unremarkable instrument of daily labor is taken up by divine commission and empowered. Jesus likewise works through the ordinary: water, bread, spit, clay. The twelve are sent with no special equipment (Matt 10:9-10), yet empowered for extraordinary works. The staff also foreshadows the cross, the instrument of Roman execution repurposed as the instrument of universal salvation.
4.3: A type: the staff thrown down becomes a serpent, recalling the serpent of Eden and anticipating the bronze serpent of Num 21. Jesus cites the wilderness serpent-on-a-pole episode directly as a type of his own crucifixion: 'as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life' (John 3:14-15). The serpent that bites is lifted up on a pole so that all who look to it may live — the one who became sin for us (2 Cor 5:21) is lifted up on the cross for the life of the world.
4.4: Moses seizes the serpent by the tail at YHWH's command, and it becomes a staff again — the reversal of death and chaos back into an instrument of life and order. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate reversal: the serpent's sting (death) is swallowed up in victory (1 Cor 15:54-57). The one who had the power of death is disarmed (Heb 2:14); the instrument of death becomes the staff of the Good Shepherd who leads his people to living water (Rev 7:17).
4.5: The purpose of the signs is faith: 'that they may believe that YHWH, the God of their fathers, has appeared to you.' The signs create and strengthen faith in the covenant God's active presence. John's Gospel is structured around seven signs (semeia), each designed to produce or deepen faith in Jesus as the Son of God (John 20:31). The goal of Exodus signs and Gospel signs is the same: not amazement at power, but trust in the Person behind the power.
4.6: The hand-in-cloak sign: healthy flesh becomes leprous, then restored. Leprosy in the OT is the paradigmatic condition of uncleanness — exclusion from the community, from worship, from touch. Jesus's healing of lepers (Matt 8:1-4; Luke 17:11-19) repeatedly crosses the boundary that leprosy enforced. The restored hand in this sign anticipates YHWH's power to reverse the uncleanness that separates humanity from his presence — a reversal completed in Christ who 'touched the leper' and cleansed him.
4.7: Flesh restored from leprous to healthy — the reversal complete. The cleansing of leprosy is explicitly Christological in the Gospel accounts: when Jesus cleanses ten lepers and one returns to give thanks, Jesus notes he is a Samaritan (Luke 17:16), foreshadowing the gospel's reach to the Gentiles. The priestly procedure for cleansing leprosy in Leviticus 14 (two birds, blood, water, hyssop, scarlet thread) is itself a complex type of death, resurrection, and cleansing that the NT applies to Christ.
4.8: Escalating signs for escalating unbelief — if the first does not convince, the second may. The sign-structure here mirrors John's Gospel, where signs are given to provoke faith but also reveal hardness of heart (John 12:37-40). Jesus performs greater and greater works, yet unbelief persists. The Exodus pattern teaches that miracles alone do not produce saving faith; only the Spirit's work through the word creates genuine trust (John 6:63-65).
4.9: The third sign: Nile water poured on dry ground becomes blood. This anticipates the first plague (Exod 7:14-24) and ultimately points to the blood of Christ — the blood of the new covenant poured out for many (Matt 26:28). Water-to-blood as a sign of divine judgment is later reversed in the sign of water-from-Jesus's-side at the cross (John 19:34), where the blood of judgment and the water of cleansing flow together from the pierced Christ.
4.10: Moses's second objection: eloquence deficiency. Paul deliberately echoes this pattern: 'I did not come to you with eloquence or human wisdom... I resolved to know nothing except Jesus Christ and him crucified... in weakness and fear and much trembling' (1 Cor 2:1-3). The inadequate speaker is the consistent type of God's commissioned agent; the power is of God, not the messenger. Jesus himself, the Word of God, was despised for the ordinariness of his Galilean speech (Matt 26:73).
4.11: YHWH declares himself the maker of the human mouth, the giver of sight and hearing. Jesus fulfills this claim directly: he heals the deaf, gives sight to the blind, looses the tongues of the mute (Matt 11:5, citing Isa 35:5-6 as messianic credentials). The one who made the mouth comes in flesh to be the Word; the one who gave sight heals blindness as a sign of his identity as the light of the world (John 9:5-7).
4.12: 'I will help you speak and will teach you what to say.' The promise of divinely given words for the divine messenger is later deepened in Jesus's promise to the disciples: 'Do not worry about what to say or how to say it... it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you' (Matt 10:19-20). The Spirit of Christ given at Pentecost is the fulfillment of the Mosaic promise — the Paraclete who brings to mind all that Jesus taught (John 14:26).
4.13: 'Please send someone else' — Moses's resistance to the commission. The Christological contrast is sharp: Jesus does not resist but embraces the commission, 'Not my will, but yours be done' (Luke 22:42). In Gethsemane, where Moses said 'send another,' Jesus said 'nevertheless, not as I will.' The author of Hebrews makes the obedience of the Son the theological center: 'I have come to do your will, O God' (Heb 10:7, quoting Ps 40:8).
4.14: YHWH's anger at Moses's reluctance, then the provision of Aaron as his spokesman. Moses as prophet-deliverer, Aaron as priestly voice — the two roles that will be unified in Christ, who is both prophet and priest (Heb 3:1; 4:14). The division of roles in Moses/Aaron points to a coming mediator who requires no imperfect human proxy because he is himself the Word of God incarnate and the perfect High Priest.
4.15: 'I will help both of you speak and will teach you what to do' — the two-person mediatorial team receives shared divine instruction. In Christ, the division between prophetic word and priestly action is overcome: he both speaks the Father's word and performs the Father's work (John 14:10), requiring no Aaron to speak what he cannot. The unity of word and deed in Jesus is what makes him the perfect mediator (1 Tim 2:5).
4.16: 'He will speak to the people for you, and it will be as if he were your mouth and as if you were God to him.' Moses functions as 'god' to Aaron — a type of divine-human mediation. Paul in 2 Cor 5:20 describes the apostolic role: 'We are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us' — humans speaking for God, just as Aaron spoke for Moses. But the antitype is greater: Jesus does not need a human Aaron because he himself is the Word made flesh, his own mouth.
4.17: The staff of God — the shepherd's staff consecrated as the instrument of divine power in the Exodus. Jesus as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11-14) comes with authority to perform mighty works not by an external instrument but by his own word and touch. The 'staff of God' becomes, in the NT, the cross — the instrument through which the divine power of resurrection and salvation is exercised.
4.18: Moses returns to Jethro and asks leave to return to Egypt to see if his people still live. The departure of the divine agent from the place of safety to enter the domain of the enemy is the structure of the incarnation: Jesus 'came down from heaven' (John 6:38), leaving the Father's presence to enter the domain of sin and death. Moses returns to Egypt; Jesus enters the world where the enemy holds people captive.
4.19: A direct type: 'All those who wanted to kill you are dead.' Matthew 2:20 quotes this verse almost verbatim — the angel's instruction to Joseph in Egypt is 'those who were trying to take the child's life are dead,' using language that unmistakably echoes Exod 4:19. The parallel identifies Jesus as the new Moses: as Moses was preserved from Pharaoh's murderous intent and called out of Egypt when it was safe, Jesus is preserved from Herod and called out of Egypt (Matt 2:15, citing Hos 11:1).
4.20: Moses takes his wife and sons on a donkey for the return journey to Egypt. The donkey connects forward to Jesus's entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (Matt 21:5, fulfilling Zech 9:9) — the gentle king who comes not on a warhorse but on a beast of burden, as the one who carries the burdens of his people. The return from the place of safety to the place of confrontation is the structure of both Moses's return to Egypt and Jesus's final journey to Jerusalem.
4.21: YHWH tells Moses he will harden Pharaoh's heart — 'chazaq' (strengthen/harden). The hardening of Pharaoh is the great OT case study in divine sovereignty and human responsibility. Paul in Romans 9:17-18 cites Pharaoh as the definitive example: 'For this purpose I raised you up, to display my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.' The exodus signs are not merely demonstrations of power but revelations of divine glory through the contrast of sovereign grace and hardened resistance.
4.22: A direct type and foundational declaration: 'Israel is my firstborn son.' This is the first explicit father-son language for the covenant people in the Torah, and it generates the typological chain: Israel as son-of-God → Jesus as the true Son of God. Matthew 2:15 cites Hos 11:1 ('Out of Egypt I called my son,' itself citing this Exodus pattern) as fulfilled in Jesus's return from Egypt after Herod's death. Israel is the type; Jesus is the antitype — the true and obedient Son who succeeds where national Israel failed (Matt 3:17; 4:1-11, where the temptations recapitulate the wilderness testing).
4.23: 'I told you: let my son go so he may serve me. But you refused; so I will kill your firstborn son.' The exchange pattern at the heart of the Exodus: Pharaoh withholds YHWH's firstborn (Israel), so YHWH will take Egypt's firstborn. This life-for-life, firstborn-for-firstborn logic reaches its culmination at the cross: God gives his only Son (John 3:16), the true Firstborn over all creation (Col 1:15), as a substitutionary offering. 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Rom 8:32) deliberately echoes the Genesis 22 pattern of the firstborn not spared.
4.24: The mysterious night attack: 'At a lodging place on the way, the LORD met Moses and was about to kill him.' The sudden divine threat against the covenant agent before his mission fulfillment is one of the OT's most enigmatic passages. Theologically, it foreshadows the pattern that the deliverer himself must pass through mortal jeopardy — must face death — before accomplishing the deliverance. Jesus in Gethsemane faced the cup of death (Matt 26:38-39) and the weight of divine judgment before the cross that accomplished salvation.
4.25: Zipporah's action: she circumcises her son and touches Moses with the foreskin, declaring 'you are a bridegroom of blood to me.' Circumcision is the covenant sign of cutting — a shedding of blood that enacts entrance into the covenant. The 'bridegroom of blood' phrase anticipates Christ as the one who, through the shedding of his blood, becomes the husband of the church (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7). The covenant community is constituted by blood — in the OT, circumcision; in the NT, the blood of Christ that seals the new covenant (Heb 9:15-18).
4.26: 'So the LORD let him alone. At that time she said bridegroom of blood, referring to circumcision.' The atonement is accomplished through blood: the threat is averted, the agent released to continue the mission. The pattern — mortal threat, blood shed, danger averted, mission continues — is the structure of the passion and resurrection. Christ faces death, sheds his blood, and the debt is paid; he is 'released' into resurrection and continues his mediatorial mission at the Father's right hand (Heb 7:25).
4.27: Aaron meets Moses at the mountain of God and kisses him. The reunion of the two brothers at the holy mountain is a scene of covenant fellowship restored. The mountain of God (Horeb/Sinai) is the locus of divine encounter throughout Exodus. Jesus's Transfiguration on a high mountain (Matt 17:1-8) represents the eschatological fulfillment of the mountain-of-God encounters: the divine glory is no longer mediated through thunder and cloud but fully revealed in the person of the Son.
4.28: Moses tells Aaron everything YHWH had said and all the signs he had commanded. The perfect transmission of the divine message — the mediator communicates fully and faithfully. Jesus declares in John 17:8: 'I have given them the words you gave me, and they accepted them.' The faithful transmission of the divine word is the central act of the prophetic and apostolic mission; Christ is the fullness of that transmission, for he is the Word that the Father speaks (John 1:1-2).
4.29: Moses and Aaron gather the elders of Israel — the covenant assembly is constituted around the word and the signs. The church as the new covenant assembly (ekklesia — called-out assembly) is gathered by Christ through the apostolic proclamation (Matt 16:18; Eph 2:20). The elders gathered before the exodus correspond to the disciples gathered before the final exodus that Jesus accomplishes in Jerusalem (Luke 9:31).
4.30: Aaron performs the signs before the people. The signs serve the word — they do not replace it but authenticate it. Paul in Romans 15:18-19 describes the apostolic mission as 'word and deed... the power of signs and wonders, through the power of the Spirit,' following the pattern established in Exodus: God's word goes forth through authorized agents whose message is authenticated by accompanying signs (Mark 16:20; Heb 2:3-4).
4.31: The people believe and bow down in worship when they see the signs and hear the word. Faith followed by worship: the pattern of authentic covenant response. The crowd at the triumphal entry (Matt 21:8-9) offers acclamation; the disciples worship at the resurrection (Matt 28:17). The Exodus pattern — signs create faith, faith produces worship — is fulfilled in the church gathered around the word and the sacraments that enact the new exodus.

5.1: 'This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: let my people go, so that they may hold a festival to me in the wilderness.' The purpose of the Exodus is worship, not merely liberation: freedom from slavery is freedom for service to God. Jesus frames the same purpose: 'the Son sets you free' (John 8:36) is not freedom for self-determination but freedom from sin for righteous living. The ultimate Exodus destination is not Canaan but the presence of God — a trajectory that culminates in the new Jerusalem of Revelation 21-22 where God dwells directly with his people.
5.2: Pharaoh's response: 'Who is the LORD, that I should obey him and let Israel go? I do not know the LORD.' The Exodus narrative is structured as the answer to Pharaoh's question — the plagues are a sustained revelation of YHWH's identity and power. The same question is answered definitively in the incarnation: 'Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9). Jesus is the final answer to the nations' 'Who is the LORD?' — the plagues gave a partial answer; Christ gives the full answer.
5.3: 'Let us take a three-day journey into the wilderness to offer sacrifices to the LORD.' The three-day journey is the stated request — and the number anticipates the three days of death, burial, and resurrection. Paul in 1 Cor 15:4 notes the resurrection 'on the third day according to the Scriptures,' pointing to the deep biblical pattern in which three days marks the transition from death to life: Jonah (1:17), Isaac (Gen 22:4), Joseph's brothers (Gen 42:18). The three-day journey to worship in the wilderness becomes, in Christ, the three-day passage through death to the ultimate worship of the Father.
5.4: Pharaoh's accusation: 'Why are you taking the people away from their labor?' The liberator is accused of disturbing the established economic and social order. Jesus faces the same accusation: he is a troubler of Israel (1 Kings 18:17 pattern), one who stirs up the people (Luke 23:5). The powers that profit from human bondage resist the proclamation of liberation — which is why 'the cross is folly to those who are perishing' (1 Cor 1:18).
5.5: Pharaoh points to the people's numerousness as the reason not to let them rest. The very fruitfulness of Israel — fulfilling the Abrahamic promise of multiplication — is used as a reason for their continued exploitation. In John 11:48-50, the religious leaders cite Jesus's popularity as the reason to eliminate him: the abundance he creates threatens the existing order. The many become the pretext for oppression rather than the occasion for joy.
5.6: Pharaoh gives the order to increase the slave-drivers' pressure. The intensification of oppression in response to the liberation proclamation is a consistent pattern: after Moses appears before Pharaoh, things get worse before they get better. Jesus warned his disciples of the same dynamic: 'I am sending you out like sheep among wolves' (Matt 10:16); the proclamation of the kingdom provokes intensified opposition before final deliverance.
5.7: 'You are no longer to supply the people with straw for making bricks; let them go and gather their own straw.' The impossible demand — produce the same output with fewer resources — is the structure of legalism that the gospel exposes and refuses. The law demands obedience but does not supply the power to obey; it reveals the impossible standard. Paul in Romans 8:3-4 argues that 'what the law was powerless to do... God did by sending his own Son': the gospel supplies what the law demands, fulfilling the righteous requirement of the law in those who walk by the Spirit.
5.8: 'Require them to make the same quota of bricks as before; don't reduce it. They are lazy.' The accusation of laziness against those who are structurally prevented from meeting impossible demands is the rhetoric of oppression in every age. Spiritually, the law's accusation 'you are lazy/sinful' is technically true but provides no remedy. Christ's atonement addresses not the accusation but the structural problem: he fulfills the requirement so that those in him are not under condemnation (Rom 8:1).
5.9: 'Make the work harder for the people so that they keep working and pay no attention to lies.' The oppressor suppresses the hope of liberation by intensifying oppression. The enemy of souls uses suffering and exhaustion to make people unreceptive to the gospel message (the 'lies' of Pharaoh's framing). Luke's parable of the sower (8:14) describes how 'the worries of life' choke the word. The Exodus sequence teaches that liberation comes from outside — from YHWH's intervention — not from the people's own effort under better conditions.
5.10: The slave-drivers relay Pharaoh's cruel decree to the Israelite workers. The mediating role of human agents in the structures of oppression contrasts with the mediating role of Moses and Aaron in the structures of liberation. Jesus describes the authority of darkness (Luke 22:53) operating through human agents; his own mediation of life comes from outside the system, not through it. The gospel is a new creation, not an improvement on the old order.
5.11: 'Go get your own straw wherever you can find it, but your work will not be reduced at all.' The cruelty of the impossible demand is made explicit: the resources are withdrawn but the expectations remain. This is a type of the human condition under sin's dominion — unable to meet the divine standard, stripped of the resources to do so, yet still held accountable. Christ as the new Moses comes to supply what is lacking: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28).
5.12: The Israelites scatter across Egypt to gather stubble for straw. The scattering of the people under oppression anticipates the exile, the diaspora, and the spiritual scatter of humanity separated from God. Jesus's mission is described as gathering the scattered children of God (John 11:52): the good shepherd seeks the scattered sheep (Ezek 34:12-16; John 10:16) and gathers them into one flock.
5.13: The slave-drivers press: 'Complete the work required of you for each day.' The relentless daily quota is the image of humanity under the law's demand — endless obligation with no rest, no relief, no completion. Jesus's 'it is finished' (John 19:30) — tetelestai, the perfect tense of completion — is the ultimate answer to the endless daily quota: the debt is paid in full, the work is done, the obligation is met once for all (Heb 10:14).
5.14: Israelite overseers are beaten because the quota is not met. The suffering of the covenant people's leaders under the impossible demand is a type of the persecution of the disciples of Christ. Jesus warned: 'If they persecuted me, they will persecute you' (John 15:20). The overseers beaten for an impossible quota become a type of those who suffer for maintaining faithfulness to the divine commission under hostile human authority.
5.15: The Israelite overseers appeal directly to Pharaoh: 'Why have you treated your servants this way?' The appeal to the oppressor for justice — a plea that will be denied — mirrors the pattern of the Psalms of lament: the righteous cry out to human authority, find no relief, and are driven back to God. The cross is the ultimate instance of unjust human authority passing sentence on the righteous one — and God overturning it through resurrection.
5.16: 'Your servants are given no straw, yet we are told Make bricks! Your servants are being beaten, but the fault lies with your own people.' The complaint articulates the injustice clearly: the accusation is false, the punishment is unjust, the system is rigged. Jesus stands before Pilate as an innocent man condemned by a rigged system (Luke 23:4, 14, 22 — Pilate three times declares Jesus innocent). The protest of injustice finds its ultimate echo in the cross.
5.17: Pharaoh's answer: 'Lazy, that is what you are — lazy! That is why you keep saying, Let us go and sacrifice to the LORD.' The desire for worship is dismissed as the excuse of the idle. The hostility of worldly powers to the claim of God on human time and loyalty is constant. Jesus's accusers similarly dismiss the kingdom's urgency as delusion or sedition (Luke 23:2). The world's economy and the kingdom's economy are in fundamental conflict.
5.18: 'Now get to work. You will not be given any straw, yet you must produce your full quota of bricks.' The decree stands — impossible, unjust, crushing. The human situation without divine intervention is the situation of this verse: demands without resources, obligation without enablement. The good news of the gospel is that Christ has fulfilled the divine requirement on behalf of those who cannot (Rom 5:8-10), stepping into the impossible position and meeting the demand from within it.
5.19: The Israelite overseers realize they are in serious trouble — they are caught between Pharaoh's impossible decree and the people's inability. The trapped mediating figures — those who stand between the oppressor and the oppressed but belong to neither camp — are a type of the apostolic witness: caught between the demands of divine truth and the pressure of human hostility. Paul describes his own situation in similar terms: 'pressed on every side' (2 Cor 4:8-9), yet not crushed.
5.20: The overseers leave Pharaoh and find Moses and Aaron waiting to meet them. The liberators are present in the moment of failure and accusation — they have not abandoned the mission. Christ's resurrection appearances to the disciples after their failure and flight are the antitype: when they leave the scene of disaster (Luke 24:13ff), the risen Lord meets them on the road and interprets the suffering in the light of scripture.
5.21: 'May the LORD look on you and judge you! You have made us obnoxious to Pharaoh and his officials and have put a sword in their hands to kill us.' The deliverer is blamed for the suffering that follows the proclamation of liberation. Jesus was blamed by the religious authorities for the disruption his ministry caused (John 11:47-50); his own people called for his death. The pattern — the liberator rejected and accused by those he came to save — is John 1:11: 'He came to his own and his own did not receive him.'
5.22: Moses returns to YHWH with the question: 'Why, Lord, why have you brought trouble on this people? Is this why you sent me?' The complaint prayer of the appointed mediator who faces incomprehension and apparent failure: the mission appears to have made things worse. This is Gethsemane's question in an earlier key. Jesus in his desolation on the cross cries out Ps 22:1: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The faithful mediator's cry into the darkness is not unfaith but the deepest form of prayer — holding fast to the relationship even in the darkness of apparent abandonment.
5.23: 'Ever since I went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has brought trouble on this people, and you have not rescued them at all.' The lament is complete: obedient mission has resulted in increased suffering with no visible rescue. The cross is the extreme point of this pattern: perfect obedience to the Father's will results in death, with no visible rescue — until the third day. The Exodus teaches that the darkest point of the story precedes the breakthrough; the passion teaches that the darkest Friday precedes the brightest Sunday.

6.1: YHWH's response to Moses's lament: 'Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh: because of my mighty hand he will let them go.' The mighty hand (yad chazaqah) is the signature Exodus formula for divine power. Jesus's resurrection is the ultimate mighty act by which the enemy of souls is compelled to release the captives: 'the reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil' (1 John 3:8). The promise 'now you will see' is the promise of vindication after the lament — Easter following Gethsemane.
6.2: 'I am the LORD' (YHWH) — the bare divine name-claim, unadorned. The progression of revelation is the theme of this chapter: each level of divine self-disclosure is more complete than the last. Jesus's 'I am' declarations (John 6:35; 8:12; 10:11; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1) claim the same YHWH-name, applying it to the Son. The final self-disclosure of God takes the form of a human face (2 Cor 4:6: 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ').
6.3: 'I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac and to Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name YHWH I did not make myself fully known to them.' The progressive revelation of the divine name: El Shaddai (the God of the patriarchal promises) is now surpassed by YHWH (the God of covenant rescue). Hebrews 1:1-2 structures this same progression: 'In many and various ways God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us through his Son.' The Son is the final name — not a word about God but God's own Son who is the Word.
6.4: The covenant with the patriarchs to give them the land of Canaan is remembered. The covenant memory (zachar) activates the rescue. Paul in 2 Cor 1:20 declares: 'All the promises of God find their Yes in him' — every OT covenant promise (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, New) finds its positive fulfillment in Jesus Christ. The remembered covenant of v.4 points forward to the covenant whose mediator and fulfillment is Christ (Heb 8:6).
6.5: YHWH hears the groaning of the enslaved Israelites and remembers his covenant. The God who hears the groan and remembers the covenant is the God whose ear is not deaf to suffering (Ps 34:18). Jesus in Gethsemane and at the cross enacts this divine compassion from the inside: he takes on the groaning of the enslaved condition (Rom 8:22-23) and from within it, as the one who hears and the one who groans, accomplishes the liberation.
6.6: The seven 'I will' promises: 'I will bring you out... free you from bondage... redeem you (ga'al) with an outstretched arm (yad netuyah)... I will take you as my people... I will be your God... I will bring you to the land... I will give it to you.' The keyword ga'al (redeemed) is the kinsman-redeemer term: only a member of the family can redeem. Hebrews 2:14-17 applies this logic to the incarnation: 'Since the children share flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity... For this reason he had to be made like his brothers in every way, in order that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest... making atonement for the sins of the people.' Jesus is the kinsman-redeemer — he became flesh to acquire the right to redeem.
6.7: 'I will take you as my own people and I will be your God.' This covenant formula — 'you will be my people and I will be your God' — is the thread that runs through the entire biblical narrative, repeated and deepened at each covenant stage (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:28; 2 Cor 6:16). Revelation 21:3 is its eschatological fulfillment: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God.' Christ's work is the means by which this formula becomes permanently true.
6.8: 'I will bring you to the land I swore with uplifted hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac and to Jacob. I will give it to you as a possession.' The inheritance promise. Jesus as the heir of all things (Heb 1:2) inherits the new creation and gives it as an inheritance to his people (Matt 5:5; Rom 8:17; 1 Pet 1:4). The land of Canaan is the type; the new creation is the antitype. The gift of the inheritance is not earned but sworn — an unconditional divine promise fulfilled through the appointed heir.
6.9: Moses reports the promises but Israel does not listen 'because of their discouragement and harsh labor' — literally, 'shortness of spirit' (qotzer ruach) and 'hard service.' The crushed spirit that cannot receive good news is the spiritual condition of those under sin's bondage. Jesus came to 'bind up the brokenhearted' (Isa 61:1 — the text he reads in Luke 4:18), to release the captives whose spirits have been broken. The problem of v.9 is not unbelief in principle but incapacity born of oppression — a pastoral problem that the gospel addresses by the Spirit's liberation.
6.10: YHWH commissions Moses again despite the failure of the first attempt to reach Israel. The re-commission after failure is the pattern of the entire biblical narrative: Moses after his killing of the Egyptian, Elijah after his flight to Horeb, Peter after his denial. The risen Christ's first post-resurrection word to the disciples is re-commission: 'Go and make disciples' (Matt 28:19). The commission is not revoked by failure; it is renewed by the risen Lord.
6.11: 'Go, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his country.' The commission repeated with clarity and urgency. The divine word is not withdrawn when it meets resistance; it is reissued. Jesus at Gethsemane chooses the cup — and the commission proceeds to the cross. The word goes forth and does not return empty (Isa 55:11).
6.12: Moses's objection: 'If the Israelites will not listen to me, why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I speak with faltering lips (aral s'fatayim — uncircumcised lips)?' The idiom 'uncircumcised lips' applies the covenant sign of circumcision to the speech organ: Moses's words are, as it were, unredeemed, not set apart. The contrast with Christ could not be sharper: Jesus is the Word of God himself (John 1:1), whose words are spirit and life (John 6:63). The weakness of the flesh (uncircumcised lips) is overcome in Christ who speaks as the Father directed him (John 12:49).
6.13: YHWH speaks to Moses and Aaron about the Israelites and Pharaoh and commands them to bring the people out of Egypt. The twofold commission — to Israel and to Pharaoh — mirrors the NT apostolic commission that is both to the covenant community (building up the church) and to the powers of the world (proclaiming Christ as Lord over all). The Great Commission (Matt 28:18-20) operates in both directions: making disciples (Israel) and teaching all nations (Pharaoh-nations).
6.14: The genealogy begins with Reuben. Levitical genealogies in Exodus serve to establish the priestly lineage, authenticating Aaron's office. The genealogy of Jesus in Matthew 1 and Luke 3 similarly authenticates his identity as the legal heir of David and the biological descendent of Abraham — the legal basis for his messianic office. Both genealogies are not merely historical records but identity-establishing documents that underwrite the authority of the figure they trace.
6.15: The sons of Simeon listed. The enumeration of Simeon here alongside Reuben situates Levi within the broader tribal structure before the priestly separation is established. Paul in Galatians 3:16-17 argues that the Abrahamic promise was given 430 years before the Sinai law and is not annulled by it — the promises to the patriarchs precede and supersede the Mosaic arrangement. The genealogy traces the human channels of promise, which find their final heir in Christ.
6.16: 'These were the names of the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.' The Levitical line is established. Hebrews 7:9-10 uses the Levitical genealogy for a remarkable theological argument: Levi paid tithes to Melchizedek through Abraham (since Levi was 'still in the loins' of Abraham). If the Levitical priesthood — established in this very genealogy — is itself subordinate to Melchizedek's order, then Christ's priesthood in the order of Melchizedek (Ps 110:4) surpasses the Levitical order that Exod 6:16 here traces.
6.17: The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei. These clan names fill out the Levitical clan structure that organizes Israel's worship throughout the wilderness period. In the new covenant, there is no Levitical clan system mediating access to God; 'there is one God and one mediator between God and humanity, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Tim 2:5). The complexity of the Levitical structure is both honored and transcended in the single perfect mediator.
6.18: The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. The Kohathites are the family that carries the most holy objects of the tabernacle (Num 4:4-20). They carry the ark, the table, the lampstand, and the altars — covered and wrapped, never touching them directly, or they die (Num 4:15). The holy objects must not be handled directly by fallen human hands. Christ as the true ark (the mercy seat — Rom 3:25), the true light of the world (John 8:12), and the true altar (Heb 13:10) is approached not through the mediation of Kohathite priests but directly through faith.
6.19: The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. The Merarites are responsible for the tabernacle's structural elements — boards, bars, posts, bases. The physical structure of YHWH's dwelling place among his people requires laborers for the frame. Christ as the cornerstone (Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:6-7) is the structural element on which the whole edifice of the church rests — not a priest carrying holy objects but the foundation that makes the building possible.
6.20: Amram married Jochebed his father's sister, and she bore Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years. The parentage of Moses and Aaron is here given official genealogical status. The priestly dynasty originates in a specific historical family. Jesus's genealogy in Luke 3 traces back through all generations to Adam — emphasizing his solidarity with all humanity — while Matthew's begins with Abraham, emphasizing his fulfillment of covenant promise. The genealogies establish the human credentials of the one whose divine identity needs no credential.
6.21: The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri. Korah will later lead the rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Num 16) — the established priestly line itself becomes the source of sacerdotal rebellion. Jude 11 cites 'Korah's rebellion' as the type of those who abandon the divine order for self-appointed leadership. The failure within the priestly line points to the need for a priest of a different order, one who holds his office permanently 'because he always lives to intercede' (Heb 7:25).
6.22: The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri. Elzaphan son of Uzziel is later commanded to carry the bodies of Nadab and Abihu from the sanctuary after their unauthorized fire (Lev 10:4-5). The priestly family is implicated in both faithful service and the consequences of sacrilegious innovation. The failed and fallible human priesthood constantly requires a succession of replacements — pointing forward to the one who 'holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever' (Heb 7:24).
6.23: Aaron married Elisheba daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon, who bore Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Nahshon son of Amminadab appears in the genealogies of Matthew 1:4 and Luke 3:32-33 as an ancestor of David and thus of Jesus. The family connection between the high-priestly line (Aaron-Elisheba) and the Davidic messianic line (Nahshon-Amminadab) is genealogically embedded here — a hint of the coming one who unites in himself the royal and priestly offices (Ps 110; Zech 6:13; Heb 7:1-3).
6.24: The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph. The Korahites, despite their ancestor's rebellion, become honored temple servants — 'the sons of Korah' compose Psalms 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88. Divine grace reverses judgment within families: the descendants of a rebel become worshippers. The cross is the ultimate instance of this reversal — the very instrument of death becomes the seat of redemption; the one executed as a criminal is Lord of all.
6.25: Eleazar son of Aaron married a daughter of Putiel and she bore Phinehas. These were the heads of the Levitical families. Phinehas will be credited with righteousness for his zealous action at Baal Peor (Num 25:7-13; Ps 106:30-31), demonstrating that zeal for YHWH's honor is itself a form of covenant faithfulness. Paul in Romans 10:2-4 distinguishes zeal without knowledge (which misses Christ) from the 'righteousness that comes from God' received through faith — the trajectory from Phinehas's zeal to Christ's perfect righteousness.
6.26: 'It was this same Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said: bring the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions.' The validation of the commission — these are the authorized agents. Jesus at the beginning of his ministry receives divine validation: 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (Matt 3:17). The Father's voice authenticates the mission before it begins; the genealogy here authenticates Moses and Aaron before the plagues and the exodus. Authorization precedes execution.
6.27: 'They were the ones who spoke to Pharaoh about bringing the Israelites out — this same Moses and Aaron.' The identification is emphatic: these specific individuals, not generic types. The incarnation is equally specific: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14) — a particular person, at a particular time, in a particular place. Salvation comes through specific people commissioned by God, not through general religious ideas. The specificity of Moses-and-Aaron typifies the specificity of Jesus of Nazareth as the one mediator.
6.28: 'On the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt.' The temporal anchor: the divine word spoken on a specific day. John's Gospel begins with 'In the beginning was the Word' — the eternal Word who enters time. The day YHWH spoke to Moses is a type of the day the Word became flesh (John 1:14) — the eternal divine speech taking temporal, historical form through a specific human agent.
6.29: 'I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I tell you.' The commission formula restated: the identity declaration (I am YHWH) followed by the imperative of total transmission. Jesus says in John 12:49-50: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken.' The perfect prophet-mediator transmits everything — nothing withheld, nothing added. Moses's total commission from YHWH is fulfilled in Jesus's total transmission of the Father's words.
6.30: 'Since I speak with faltering lips, why would Pharaoh listen to me?' Moses's objection returns — the chapter ends where it began (v.12), framing the genealogy with the problem of inadequate human agency. The answer to this question is the entire gospel narrative: because the Word became flesh (John 1:14), because God sent his own Son (Rom 8:3), the message goes forth not in the weakness of a reluctant human speaker but in the power of the eternal Word who addresses Pharaoh-the-world through the weakness of the cross (1 Cor 1:25).

7.1: YHWH makes Moses 'elohim to Pharaoh' — a god-like figure before the earthly ruler. The word elohim here assigns Moses a divine representative role: he speaks for YHWH to the ruler of the most powerful empire on earth. This prefigures Christ's role as the definitive Word of God confronting the 'ruler of this world' (John 12:31). As Moses is YHWH's elohim-figure to Pharaoh, so Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' (Col 1:15) who confronts and defeats every power that claims sovereignty over humanity.
7.2: Moses speaks everything YHWH commands; Aaron speaks to Pharaoh. The mediatorial chain — YHWH → Moses → Aaron → Pharaoh — anticipates the NT pattern where the divine word passes through the Son to the world. Jesus is both Moses and Aaron in one: he is both the one who receives the Father's word perfectly (John 8:28) and the one who speaks it directly to the world without an intermediary. The doubled mediation of the exodus gives way to the single perfect Mediator (1 Tim 2:5).
7.3: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart (ani aksheh et-lev par'oh).' The divine hardening is already announced before the plague sequence begins. Theologically, this anticipates Paul's treatment of divine sovereignty and human responsibility in Rom 9:17–18, where he quotes Exod 9:16 and cites Pharaoh as the paradigmatic case of divine governance over the obstinate. The hardened heart is the condition of those who resist the word of God persistently — a condition that only Christ's new-covenant work reverses (Ezek 36:26; 2 Cor 3:14–16).
7.4: 'Egypt will not listen to you' — the refusal of the word is announced in advance. This matches the Johannine prologue pattern: 'He came to his own and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). Jesus taught in parables partially because of the same dynamic — the word spoken to a hardened audience increases their accountability without converting them (Matt 13:13–15, quoting Isa 6). The exodus refuses and the ministry refuses are structurally parallel: a nation's rejection of YHWH's messenger leads to judgment.
7.5: 'The Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH when I stretch out my hand over Egypt.' The goal of the plagues is revelatory — YHWH's identity as sovereign Lord is to be known through the judgment. This knowledge-through-judgment formula anticipates the cross: it was through the seeming defeat of crucifixion that 'all peoples' would be drawn to acknowledge the crucified one (John 12:32; Phil 2:9–11). Both at the Red Sea and at Golgotha, YHWH's identity as Lord is revealed precisely in the act of confronting and defeating the powers.
7.6: Moses and Aaron did as YHWH commanded — exactly. The obedience formula ka'asher tzivah YHWH is unqualified. This complete covenant obedience mirrors Christ's perfect obedience to the Father: 'I always do the things that are pleasing to him' (John 8:29). The exodus mediators' obedience is a type of the one who was 'obedient to the point of death' (Phil 2:8) and through whose obedience 'many will be made righteous' (Rom 5:19).
7.7: Moses was eighty, Aaron eighty-three when they spoke to Pharaoh. The age detail grounds the narrative historically but also points forward: the deliverer came not in the vigor of youth but in the fullness of time and preparation. The NT analogue is not age-literal but pattern-significant: Christ came 'when the fullness of time had come' (Gal 4:4) — the timing of both deliverers was entirely determined by YHWH, not by the deliverers themselves.
7.8: YHWH speaks to Moses and Aaron: when Pharaoh asks for a sign, Aaron is to throw his staff down. The invitation to demonstrate divine power sets up the first confrontation between YHWH's representatives and the court magicians. This is the paradigm of every encounter between the word of God and the wisdom of the world — the world demands signs (1 Cor 1:22), and YHWH provides a demonstration that cannot ultimately be matched.
7.9: Aaron throws down his staff and it becomes a tanin — a serpent/sea-monster. The same word is used in Gen 1:21 for the great sea creatures and in Ezek 29:3 for Pharaoh himself as 'the great dragon lying in the Nile.' The staff-serpent confrontation is cosmic: YHWH's rod becomes the creature that Pharaoh embodies. The one who would crush the serpent (Gen 3:15) acts first through a sign that makes the serpent serve YHWH's purpose.
7.10: Aaron throws his staff before Pharaoh and his servants and it becomes a serpent — the sign performed publicly, before the highest court. The public context matters: the defeat of the serpent will not happen in private. Both the plagues and the cross are public acts — YHWH's power displayed before witnesses. 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Col 2:15). The open display of power over the serpent begins here.
7.11: Pharaoh's wise men and sorcerers (chakhamim, mekashefim) replicate the sign with their secret arts. The magicians can produce serpents too — false signs that superficially match YHWH's. This is the NT's persistent warning about counterfeit signs: 'False christs and false prophets will arise and perform great signs and wonders, so as to lead astray, if possible, even the elect' (Matt 24:24). The ability to perform signs is not in itself proof of divine authorization.
7.12: Aaron's staff swallowed (vayivla) all the magicians' staffs. The decisive sign: YHWH's serpent consumes the imitations. This is the first act of the plague sequence but theologically the most important: the enemy's power is not denied but absorbed and overcome. The cross follows the same logic — death is swallowed up in victory (1 Cor 15:54, quoting Isa 25:8). The enemy's best weapon (the serpent, death) is swallowed by the one it tried to destroy.
7.13: Pharaoh's heart was hardened and he did not listen. The first hardening confirms the pattern established in v. 3. The cumulative effect of Pharaoh's refusals forms a theological argument: persistent rejection of YHWH's word hardens the heart further with each refusal. Paul uses this same logic when he describes those who suppress the truth (Rom 1:21–28) — each rejection intensifies the blindness it produces.
7.14: YHWH tells Moses: 'Pharaoh's heart is heavy (kaved) — he refuses to let the people go.' The kaved (heavy/hardened) heart of Pharaoh and the kavod (glory/heaviness) of YHWH share the same root — the display of YHWH's glory is directly linked to overcoming the human heart's heaviness. What glorifies YHWH most is the deliverance of people from the condition that has made them spiritually heavy and unresponsive.
7.15: YHWH instructs Moses to go to Pharaoh at the Nile in the morning — the Nile where Pharaoh went regularly. Pharaoh worshipped the Nile or monitored it as a divine resource; YHWH meets him at the place of his false security. This is the pattern of prophetic confrontation: the prophet meets the king at the place that represents his self-sufficiency.
